title: Cardiac Tamponade
oneLiner: Life-threatening compression of the heart by pericardial fluid → obstructive shock; requires urgent drainage to restore cardiac output.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** (mimics tamponade with hypotension + distended neck veins): the single finding that says “not tamponade” is **absent breath sounds** on the affected side (tamponade has clear lungs).
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** (also causes shock + raised JVP): the single finding that says “not tamponade” is **acute right ventricular strain on ECG** (S1Q3T3, RBBB, P pulmonale) plus **hypoxia** with clear CXR – tamponade typically has low voltage, not acute RV strain.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dyspnoea, chest discomfort (pressure), presyncope/syncope, fatigue, nausea  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) – present in <30% but highly specific  
  - Pulsus paradoxus (inspiratory fall in systolic BP >10 mmHg) – measure manually  
  - Kussmaul sign (rising JVP on inspiration)  
  - Tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure, cool peripheries
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute pericarditis** (pain positional, relieved by sitting forward, ECG diffuse ST elevation + PR depression, no hypotension/paradoxus)  
- **Tension pneumothorax** (absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation, abrupt onset after trauma/invasive procedure)  
- **Massive PE** (hypoxia, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs, acute RV strain on ECG, normal JVP waveform)  
- **Acute heart failure** (pulmonary oedema, crackles, S3 gallop, cardiomegaly on CXR, BNP >400 ng/L)  
- **Splitter:** **Presence of pulsus paradoxus** – strongly favours tamponade over pericarditis (rare) or PE (absent). Measure with manual BP cuff.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - 12-lead ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans, sinus tachycardia)  
  - Bedside echo (if available) – pericardial effusion, right atrial/ventricular collapse in diastole, IVC dilatation with minimal respiratory variation  
  - Manual BP check for pulsus paradoxus  
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, coagulation screen, troponin, BNP, lactate, blood cultures (if infection suspected)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Echocardiogram** – transthoracic (TTE) will show effusion and tamponade physiology  
  - Second-line: CT chest – if echo is equivocal or suspicion remains high (e.g., post-cardiac surgery, localized effusion)  
- **Special tests:** Pericardial fluid analysis (cell count, culture, cytology, ADA) – guided by cause  
- **Never-miss test:** **Bedside echocardiogram** (or urgent formal TTE) in any hypotensive patient with distended neck veins and clear lungs – its omission is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Call cardiology / cardiac surgery / emergency physician for **urgent pericardiocentesis** (echo-guided if available; blind sub-xiphoid approach if unstable)  
  - IV fluid bolus (500-1000 mL crystalloid cautious – to maintain preload, but avoid overload)  
  - Avoid positive pressure ventilation if possible (reduces venous return, worsens shock)  
  - Do not delay drainage for imaging if patient in extremis  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Monitor for reaccumulation (repeat echo within 24h of drainage)  
  - Treat underlying cause:  
    - Idiopathic/viral: NSAIDs + colchicine (if pericarditis)  
    - Bacterial: IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin or ceftriaxone + vancomycin if surgical)  
    - Malignant: consider pericardial window, oncology referral  
  - Daily symptom review and vital signs/NEWS2 every hour for 4h, then 4-hourly if stable  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If recurrent → surgical pericardiotomy / pericardial window  
  - Oncology follow-up for malignant effusions  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline IV 1 L/24h (if no pulmonary congestion)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (hold if high bleeding risk post-drainage – discuss with senior)  
  - NBM until plan for pericardiocentesis is clear  
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15 min initially if unstable, then hourly for 4h, 4-hourly after stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (if associated pericarditis):**  
  - Ibuprofen 600 mg PO TDS (or Aspirin 2.4–4 g/day in divided doses) – continue for 2 weeks  
  - Colchicine 500 micrograms PO BD for 6 weeks (reduce dose if age >70 or renal impairment: 500 micrograms OD)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD  
  - Prednisolone (avoid unless refractory or autoimmune cause – may promote recurrence)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if AKI, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), peptic ulcer, or recent GI bleed  
  - Colchicine: caution with renal/hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic disease; interacts with macrolides, statins (increases myopathy risk)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Colchicine: if eGFR 30–50 → reduce to 500 micrograms OD; if <30 → avoid  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving anticoagulants (e.g., therapeutic LMWH, DOACs) before ruling out haemorrhagic cause of tamponade** – can cause catastrophic reaccumulation. Always check coagulation, platelets, and cause first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Cardiac injury (myocardial puncture, coronary artery laceration) during pericardiocentesis  
  - Pneumothorax (if sub-xiphoid approach)  
  - Reaccumulation of fluid (especially if malignant or inflammatory)  
  - Infection (pericarditis → purulent pericarditis / pericardial abscess)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Constrictive pericarditis (fibrous thickening → restrictive physiology) – particularly after TB, purulent, or radiation-induced pericarditis
sections.Prognosis:
- If promptly diagnosed and drained (within hours of shock), mortality <10%, full recovery expected.  
- Malignant tamponade carries poor long-term prognosis (median survival months, depending on type of cancer).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform bedside echo** in any hypotensive patient with distended neck veins and clear lungs – it’s the quickest way to rule in/out tamponade.  
- **Measure pulsus paradoxus manually** (BP cuff + careful auscultation): a drop >10 mmHg with inspiration is diagnostic when combined with other signs.  
- **Consider tamponade in post-cardiac surgery patients** (even with drains in situ – localized effusions can still cause compression).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on CXR alone** (globular heart is insensitive; may be normal in acute onset).  
- **Avoid positive pressure ventilation** in suspected tamponade – it reduces venous return and can precipitate cardiac arrest. Use simple mask oxygen instead if possible.  
- **Do not drain a small effusion without haemodynamic compromise** – pericardiocentesis carries risks; only drain if tamponade physiology is confirmed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, or syncope  
  - Fever or rigors (suggest infection)  
  - Reappearance of neck vein distension or hypotension  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks (or by local protocol)  
  - Repeat echo in 4–6 weeks to check for reaccumulation
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >25, SpO2 <90%, systolic BP <90 mmHg)  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS <13 or any deterioration in consciousness  
  - Signs of obstructive shock persisting after initial fluid bolus (BP still low, JVP high)  
- **Direct referral to cardiology / cardiac surgery** for urgent pericardiocentesis (aim within 30 minutes of diagnosis if unstable).

rawMarkdown:
## Cardiac Tamponade
**One-liner:** Life-threatening compression of the heart by pericardial fluid → obstructive shock; requires urgent drainage to restore cardiac output.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax** (mimics tamponade with hypotension + distended neck veins): the single finding that says “not tamponade” is **absent breath sounds** on the affected side (tamponade has clear lungs).
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** (also causes shock + raised JVP): the single finding that says “not tamponade” is **acute right ventricular strain on ECG** (S1Q3T3, RBBB, P pulmonale) plus **hypoxia** with clear CXR – tamponade typically has low voltage, not acute RV strain.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dyspnoea, chest discomfort (pressure), presyncope/syncope, fatigue, nausea  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) – present in <30% but highly specific  
  - Pulsus paradoxus (inspiratory fall in systolic BP >10 mmHg) – measure manually  
  - Kussmaul sign (rising JVP on inspiration)  
  - Tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure, cool peripheries

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute pericarditis** (pain positional, relieved by sitting forward, ECG diffuse ST elevation + PR depression, no hypotension/paradoxus)  
- **Tension pneumothorax** (absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation, abrupt onset after trauma/invasive procedure)  
- **Massive PE** (hypoxia, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs, acute RV strain on ECG, normal JVP waveform)  
- **Acute heart failure** (pulmonary oedema, crackles, S3 gallop, cardiomegaly on CXR, BNP >400 ng/L)  
- **Splitter:** **Presence of pulsus paradoxus** – strongly favours tamponade over pericarditis (rare) or PE (absent). Measure with manual BP cuff.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - 12-lead ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans, sinus tachycardia)  
  - Bedside echo (if available) – pericardial effusion, right atrial/ventricular collapse in diastole, IVC dilatation with minimal respiratory variation  
  - Manual BP check for pulsus paradoxus  
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, coagulation screen, troponin, BNP, lactate, blood cultures (if infection suspected)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Echocardiogram** – transthoracic (TTE) will show effusion and tamponade physiology  
  - Second-line: CT chest – if echo is equivocal or suspicion remains high (e.g., post-cardiac surgery, localized effusion)  
- **Special tests:** Pericardial fluid analysis (cell count, culture, cytology, ADA) – guided by cause  
- **Never-miss test:** **Bedside echocardiogram** (or urgent formal TTE) in any hypotensive patient with distended neck veins and clear lungs – its omission is a serious error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Call cardiology / cardiac surgery / emergency physician for **urgent pericardiocentesis** (echo-guided if available; blind sub-xiphoid approach if unstable)  
  - IV fluid bolus (500-1000 mL crystalloid cautious – to maintain preload, but avoid overload)  
  - Avoid positive pressure ventilation if possible (reduces venous return, worsens shock)  
  - Do not delay drainage for imaging if patient in extremis  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Monitor for reaccumulation (repeat echo within 24h of drainage)  
  - Treat underlying cause:  
    - Idiopathic/viral: NSAIDs + colchicine (if pericarditis)  
    - Bacterial: IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin or ceftriaxone + vancomycin if surgical)  
    - Malignant: consider pericardial window, oncology referral  
  - Daily symptom review and vital signs/NEWS2 every hour for 4h, then 4-hourly if stable  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If recurrent → surgical pericardiotomy / pericardial window  
  - Oncology follow-up for malignant effusions  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline IV 1 L/24h (if no pulmonary congestion)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (hold if high bleeding risk post-drainage – discuss with senior)  
  - NBM until plan for pericardiocentesis is clear  
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15 min initially if unstable, then hourly for 4h, 4-hourly after stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (if associated pericarditis):**  
  - Ibuprofen 600 mg PO TDS (or Aspirin 2.4–4 g/day in divided doses) – continue for 2 weeks  
  - Colchicine 500 micrograms PO BD for 6 weeks (reduce dose if age >70 or renal impairment: 500 micrograms OD)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD  
  - Prednisolone (avoid unless refractory or autoimmune cause – may promote recurrence)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if AKI, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), peptic ulcer, or recent GI bleed  
  - Colchicine: caution with renal/hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic disease; interacts with macrolides, statins (increases myopathy risk)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Colchicine: if eGFR 30–50 → reduce to 500 micrograms OD; if <30 → avoid  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving anticoagulants (e.g., therapeutic LMWH, DOACs) before ruling out haemorrhagic cause of tamponade** – can cause catastrophic reaccumulation. Always check coagulation, platelets, and cause first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Cardiac injury (myocardial puncture, coronary artery laceration) during pericardiocentesis  
  - Pneumothorax (if sub-xiphoid approach)  
  - Reaccumulation of fluid (especially if malignant or inflammatory)  
  - Infection (pericarditis → purulent pericarditis / pericardial abscess)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Constrictive pericarditis (fibrous thickening → restrictive physiology) – particularly after TB, purulent, or radiation-induced pericarditis

### Prognosis
- If promptly diagnosed and drained (within hours of shock), mortality <10%, full recovery expected.  
- Malignant tamponade carries poor long-term prognosis (median survival months, depending on type of cancer).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform bedside echo** in any hypotensive patient with distended neck veins and clear lungs – it’s the quickest way to rule in/out tamponade.  
- **Measure pulsus paradoxus manually** (BP cuff + careful auscultation): a drop >10 mmHg with inspiration is diagnostic when combined with other signs.  
- **Consider tamponade in post-cardiac surgery patients** (even with drains in situ – localized effusions can still cause compression).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on CXR alone** (globular heart is insensitive; may be normal in acute onset).  
- **Avoid positive pressure ventilation** in suspected tamponade – it reduces venous return and can precipitate cardiac arrest. Use simple mask oxygen instead if possible.  
- **Do not drain a small effusion without haemodynamic compromise** – pericardiocentesis carries risks; only drain if tamponade physiology is confirmed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, or syncope  
  - Fever or rigors (suggest infection)  
  - Reappearance of neck vein distension or hypotension  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks (or by local protocol)  
  - Repeat echo in 4–6 weeks to check for reaccumulation

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >25, SpO2 <90%, systolic BP <90 mmHg)  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS <13 or any deterioration in consciousness  
  - Signs of obstructive shock persisting after initial fluid bolus (BP still low, JVP high)  
- **Direct referral to cardiology / cardiac surgery** for urgent pericardiocentesis (aim within 30 minutes of diagnosis if unstable).
